records.ety: [F. dtacher (cf. It. distaccare, staccare); pref. d (L. dis) + the root found also in E. attach. See Attach, and cf. Staccato.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Detached (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Detaching.]

defs: To part; to separate or disunite; to disengage; -- the opposite of attach; as, to detach the coats of a bulbous root from each other; to detach a man from a leader or from a party.
To separate for a special object or use; -- used especially in military language; as, to detach a ship from a fleet, or a company from a regiment.
To push asunder; to come off or separate from anything; to disengage.
quotes: [A vapor] detaching, fold by fold, From those still heights.
